narrative:
Your first-year grades placed you in the top decile of your school, which is outstanding and well above the typical student's position around the middle of their class. However, your third-year grades landed you in the third decile, still solid but closer to average for most applicants. With an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, below the usual 3.21 seen in the dataset, and an LSAT score of 29.50 compared to the average 36.70, your academic profile shows some strong starts but also areas that didn't keep pace with peers. Coming from an upper-middle-class family and planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students (over 92%), those aspects align with the norm and didn't stand out either way. As a Black male applicant, your background is part of what the model considered alongside everyone else's diverse profiles in predicting bar exam outcomes.

The model estimates a 41% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your law school application to minimize the risk of poor bar passage rates.

The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your first-year decile ranking—it had the strongest influence, signaling to the model a potential vulnerability despite its strength on paper, perhaps because early performance often sets the trajectory for law school success.

Your LSAT score pulled in the opposite direction, helping to argue against failure by showing capability above what the model might expect in weaker profiles.

Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a moderate counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward passing compared to even lower GPAs it has seen.

Overall, while your early grades carried the most weight in driving the rejection, your test scores and prior GPA offered some reassurance but weren't enough to offset that top influence.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above the 36.70 average, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and highlighting any upward trends or extenuating circumstances from your third year. Reaching out to admissions advisors for feedback or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment and background could open new doors. With targeted improvements, you can reshape how models like this see your potential next time.
